coredata.dc:title: Green, energy-efficient preparation of CDs-embedded BiPO4 heterostructure for better light harvesting and conversion
coredata.dc:creator: Chang, Qing
Yang, Wenliang
Li, Fu
Xue, Chaorui
Wang, Huiqi
Li, Ning
Liu, Hantao
Yang, Jinlong
Hu, Shengliang
coredata.dc:description: To facilitate the formation of bicomponent heterostructure, here a novel strategy of surface group-mediated nucleation reaction is developed to prepare carbon dots-embedded BiPO4 heterostructure (CDs@BiPO4). The presented synthetic route is simple, fast, cheap and without the need for external heating and any additional energy input. The obtained CDs@BiPO4 exhibits a broadband absorption from 300 to 800 nm, no matter absorption intensity or visible absorption range it far exceeds the similar products prepared by energy-consuming hydrothermal method reported. The charge transfer impedance of CDs@BiPO4 is only one seventh of that of pure BiPO4. Moreover, CDs act as electron donors and trapping agent of photogenerated holes in the heterostructure. Accordingly, more photoinduced electrons are produced from CDs@BiPO4 than pure BiPO4 and can be used to activate nitro compounds, promoting their reduction with hydrogen species from borohydride ions. After CDs are embedded, the catalytic activity of BiPO4 for the reduction of 4-nitrophenol to 4-aminophenol is enhanced around 2.5-fold under visible light irradiation. Besides, the CDs@BiPO4 reveals higher stability and reusability than pure BiPO4.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Heterostructure
Photocatalysis
Carbon dots
Nucleation reaction

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of CDs@BiPO4, BiPO4, and CDs@BiPO4 (180 °C)

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) The ratio of ΔGhomo/ΔGhetero vs. × at a given m; (b) Schematic illumination of surface group-mediated nucleation reaction; (c) and (d) TEM and HRTEM images of pure BiPO4, the inset in (c) showing a lower magnification image; (e) and (f) TEM and HRTEM images of CDs@BiPO4, the inset in (e) showing the larger magnification for labeled by the red circle.
(a) Line scan profiles of Bi and C elements; the inset is the corresponding high-angle annular dark-field scanning TEM image; (b) XRD pattern of BiPO4 and CDs@BiPO4.
UV–Vis absorption spectra of BiPO4 and CDs@BiPO4 (a) and their EIS (b).

(a) Luminescence emission spectra of CDs without and with the quenchers of DNT and DEA; (b) Mott-Schottky plot; (c) Absorption spectra of NBT for
O2 − detection; (d) Fluorescence spectra of generated 2-hydroxyterephthalic acid (TAOH) in reaction system for
OH detection; (e) The energy level diagrams of CDs and BiPO4 together with charge transfer directions between with them.

(a) and (b) UV–Vis spectra of the 4-NP reduction in the presence of BiPO4 without and with visible light irradiation; (c) and (d) UV–Vis spectra of the 4-NP reduction in the presence of CDs@BiPO4 without and with visible light irradiation; (e) and (f) Reaction rate comparisons for 4-NP reduction in the presence of BiPO4 and CDs@BiPO4 without and with visible light irradiation, respectively.
Plot of conversion rate versus light intensities at the given irradiation time (a) and The effects of light wavelengths on reaction rate (b) and TOF (c).

(a) The recyclability of CDs@BiPO4 and BiPO4 for photocatalytic reduction of 4-NP at the same condition, respectively; (b) The corresponding XRD patterns of the CDs@BiPO4 and BiPO4 before used and after repeatedly used five times, respectively; (c-f) The corresponding XPS full spectra, Bi 4f, O 1s and P 2p spectra of the CDs@BiPO4 and BiPO4 before used and after repeatedly used five times, respectively (Red line: the fresh CDs@BiPO4; Blue line: the fresh BiPO4; Black line: the used CDs@BiPO4; Purple line: the used BiPO4).
Schematic illuminations for 4-NP reduction without and with visible light irradiation.

head.ce:title: Green, energy-efficient preparation of CDs-embedded BiPO4 heterostructure for better light harvesting and conversion

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: North University of China, School of Energy and Power Engineering & School of Material Science and Engineering, Taiyuan 030051, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Energy and Power Engineering & School of Material Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: North University of China, School of Energy and Power Engineering & School of Material Science and Engineering, Taiyuan 030051, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of New Ceramics and Fine Processing, Tsinghua University, Beijing 100084, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of New Ceramics and Fine Processing

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of New Ceramics and Fine Processing, Tsinghua University, Beijing 100084, PR China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: To facilitate the formation of bicomponent heterostructure, here a novel strategy of surface group-mediated nucleation reaction is developed to prepare carbon dots-embedded BiPO4 heterostructure (CDs@BiPO4). The presented synthetic route is simple, fast, cheap and without the need for external heating and any additional energy input. The obtained CDs@BiPO4 exhibits a broadband absorption from 300 to 800 nm, no matter absorption intensity or visible absorption range it far exceeds the similar products prepared by energy-consuming hydrothermal method reported. The charge transfer impedance of CDs@BiPO4 is only one seventh of that of pure BiPO4. Moreover, CDs act as electron donors and trapping agent of photogenerated holes in the heterostructure. Accordingly, more photoinduced electrons are produced from CDs@BiPO4 than pure BiPO4 and can be used to activate nitro compounds, promoting their reduction with hydrogen species from borohydride ions. After CDs are embedded, the catalytic activity of BiPO4 for the reduction of 4-nitrophenol to 4-aminophenol is enhanced around 2.5-fold under visible light irradiation. Besides, the CDs@BiPO4 reveals higher stability and reusability than pure BiPO4.

ce:section.ce:para:
Incorporating two components within individual nanostructures has attracted significant attention in controlling chemical and physical properties at the nanoscale to obtain efficient catalysts and advanced energy-conversion [1–3]. Heterogeneous nucleation at the nanoscale is widely used for synthesis of bicomponent heterostructures, e. g. the seed-mediated growth method, which allows the synthesis of heterostructures in diverse shapes, including core/shells, nanorods and nanodumbbells [1,2,4,5]. In striking contrast to single component nanostructure synthesis, the formation of multicomponent heterostructures makes the synthetic reaction more complicated. According to nucleation thermodynamics, the Gibbs free energy of a critical nucleus (with radius of R) on a spherical seed with radius r is a function of the size proportion (r/R) and the contact angle θ between the nucleus and the spherical seed. Letting x = r/R and m = cosθ, its generalized equation can be written as [6,7],
(1)
Δ G
hetero
=
16 π
γ
3
3
Δ g
2
f
( m , x )
(2)
f
m , x
=
1 2
+
1 2
1 - m x
g
3
+
1 2
x
3
2 - 3
x - m
g
+
x - m
g
3
+
3 2
m
x
2
x - m
g
- 1
(3)
g =
1 +
x
2
- 2 m x
where Δg and γ are the Gibbs free energy difference per unit volume and the surface free energy, respectively. Since the free energy of homogeneous nucleation (ΔGhomo ) can be obtained at x = 0 and f (m,x) = 1, the dependence of the ratio (ΔGhomo/ΔGhetero ) with m and x is shown in Fig. 1 a. The ratio value increases with m and x, indicating the larger the contact angle and/or the radius of the seed, the smaller the value of f (m, x) and the difference between ΔGhomo and ΔGhetero . To facilitate heterogeneous nucleation reaction or restrain homogeneous nucleation reaction, thereby we need to reduce contact angle between the nucleus and the seed at the given seed size.

Photocatalysis can be used for water splitting to hydrogen, selective organic transformations for preparation of fine chemicals, environmental remediation, and etc. [8–10]. Nevertheless, single semiconductor materials are extremely difficult to meet all ideal photocatalytic features, such as a broad light absorption, suitable band alignment to satisfy the targeted reaction, quick charge separation and high photocarrier mobility [8]. Therefore, bi- or multi-component heterostructures toward specific photocatalytic reaction open up a new promising pathway for photocatalysis development. Two-dimensional graphene possesses high specific surface area, excellent transparency, superior electron mobility, high chemical stablility, and so it has been considered as an ideal co-catalyst to improve the photocatalytic properties of semiconductors [8,11]. However, it belongs to a zero-band gap material and cannot be directly utilized for photoactive heterostructures. Besides, graphene use in photocatalysis is severely limited by its poor dispersibility and irreversible agglomeration in common solvents, which can strongly weaken its unique properties mentioned above [8,11].

By contrast, luminescent carbon dots (CDs) that are discrete carbonaceous nanoparticles with sizes below 10 nm have tunable light-harvesting range, superior dispersibility in solvents, excellent electron-transfer efficiency and outstanding photoinduced redox properties [12]. As a promising new class of metal-free photocatalyst, not only do the unique features of CDs offer many new avenues for the intelligent design of CDs-related heterostructures, but also their ease of synthesis, low cost and non-toxicity are highly advantageous for industrial use and environment sustainability in comparison with noble metal-based quantum dots and semiconductor quantum dots with the known toxicity and potential environmental hazard [9,12,13]. Accordingly, CDs have better advantages in developing multifunctional and photoactive heterostructures for the applications in solar-energy conversion and addressing environmental pollution issues [9,12–20]. A broad range of CDs-related heterostructures that combine different constituents including of metals, metal compounds and semiconductors have been fabricated till now [9,12–18], and displayed many potential applications in photocatalysis. However, the past reports have less effective approaches to regulate heterogeneous nucleation process. Although the other phase was also formed in the presence of CDs under hydrothermal conditions, nobodycanmake sure thatits nucleation reaction occurs on the surface of CDs while the size of critical nucleus is close to or larger than that of CDs.

In this work, we report a green and energy-efficient method of boosting heterostructure formation through surface group-mediated nucleation reaction, which allows for the reduction of the contact angle (θ) between the nucleus and carbon dot. We firstly employed phosphate group-grafted CDs as nucleation seeds and induced the nucleation and growth of BiPO4 on their surface under room conditions (Fig. 1b), resulting in the formation of CDs-embedded BiPO4 heterostructure (CDs@BiPO4). BiPO4 is now regarded as a representative photocatalyst with a non-metallic oxyacid structure [21,22]. Due to an induced effect inherently possessed by oxyacid anions with negative charges, the efficient separation of the photogenerated electrons and holes can be promoted in BiPO4 [21]. Therefore, it exhibits higher UV-responsive activity than P25 (TiO2) and no obvious deactivation during the photocatalytic processes. Current study on the practical use of photoactive BiPO4 is still focused on extending its absorption wavelength to the visible-light region and large-scale preparation [21–23]. Excitingly, our presented heterostructure of CDs@BiPO4 not only has low preparation cost, but also possesses a good ability of visible light harvesting and better light-energy conversion efficiency.

Phosphate group-grafted CDs were prepared by modifying coal-based CDs with diammonium phosphate (DAP) reported by us [24,25]. Typically, the CDs solution was firstly prepared through selective oxidation of coal with H2O2 solution. Subsequently, 100 mg of DAP was added in 10 mL of CDs solution and mixed with 10 mL absolute ethanol. The resultant solution was heated at 200 °C for 10 h. After cooling to room temperature, phosphate group-modified CDs were produced.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of CDs@BiPO4, BiPO4, and CDs@BiPO4 (180 °C)
ce:section.ce:para: Firstly, 367 mg Bi(NO3)3·5H2O were well dissolved in 22 mL of 0.1 M HNO3 solution, obtaining Bi(NO3)3 aqueous solution. Secondly, the resultant Bi(NO3)3 solution was added drop by drop to phosphate group-modified CDs solution under vigorousstirringconditions, and then standing for12 hours. After adjusting pH value of the mixed solution to 7–8 using ammonia water, the solid sample was collected and then washed with distilled water and ethanol to remove the unreacted ions. Finally dried in an oven at 60 °C for 2 h and then obtained CDs@BiPO4. For comparison, the mixed solution of Bi(NO3)3 and CDs solution without standing was directly transferred to a 100 mL Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave and maintained at 180 °C for 3 hours. After adjusting pH value and washed using the same method mentioned above, the other sample CDs@BiPO4 (180 °C) was obtained. The pure BiPO4 sample was simply prepared through replacing the solution of phosphate group-modified CDs by the aqueous solution of 100 mg DAP.

Phosphate group-modified CDs obtained by modifying coal-based CDs with DAP reveal relatively uniform sizes (Fig. S1). Because phosphate groups are grafted on the surface of CDs, more favorable condition making Bi3+ ions or BiPO4 nuclei accessible to the surface of CDs is created [26]. This could help to reduce the contact angle between CDs and BiPO4 (Fig. 1a). Moreover, our measured zeta potential results demonstrated that the surface of CDs was oppositely charged to BiPO4 (Fig. S2). This further implied that homogeneous nucleation reaction could be restrained in the presence of phosphate-grafted CDs. As shown in Fig. 1b, BiPO4 as the overgrowth phase on the preformed CDs grows up through Ostwald ripening and/or oriented attachment. To compare, we also prepared single BiPO4 in the absence of CDs under the same conditions. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) observations reveal a smooth rod-like nanostructure accompanying with sharp tips at the end for as-prepared BiPO4 (Fig. 1c). High resolution TEM (HRTEM) image clearly displays the crystalline structure of the BiPO4 with lattice spacing of 0.61 nm, corresponding well to the (100) lattice planes of hexagonal BiPO4 phase (Fig. 1d). In striking contrast to single BiPO4, CDs@BiPO4 shows a rod-like shape without sharp tips at the end (Fig. 1e). By further magnification of the nanorod terminal, one can observe a typical morphology feature distinguished from pure BiPO4 (the inset of Fig. 1e), displaying the incorporation of abundant black dots in the nanorod. Further HRTEM image (Fig. 1f) shows two kinds of the lattice fringes of 0.61 nm and 0.32 nm in the crystal structure of the nanorod, which are assigned to the lattice plane of hexagonal BiPO4 (100) and graphitic structure (002), respectively. As a result, the black dots observed in the low magnification image could belong to CDs embedded into the BiPO4 phase. As our description mentioned above, the reduction of the contact angle of the BiPO4 nucleus on phosphate group-modified CDs is favorable to heterogeneous nucleation reaction. The formation of a mass of heterogeneous nuclei of CDs@BiPO4 possibly gives rise to growth process induced by oriented attachment and/or Ostwald ripening and then generates the larger rod-like shape of CDs@BiPO4 [27,28]. This can be also verified by the N2 adsorption/desorption isotherms and pore size distributions, showing the generation of much more mesopores and larger Brunauer–Emmett–Teller (BET) surface area in CDs@BiPO4 than pure BiPO4 (Fig. S3).

Furthermore, the energy-dispersive spectrum (EDS) line scan analysis of CDs@BiPO4 reveals that Bi and C elements are complementary and crossing distributions along the red line (Fig. 2 a). Combining with the analysis of the morphology feature results mentioned above, it can be confirmed that CDs are embedded into BiPO4 phase. Fig. 2b depicts the X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns of the as-synthesized CDs@BiPO4 and pure BiPO4. All of the diffraction peaks in the XRD pattern of CDs@BiPO4 could be indexed into the hexagonal phase of pure BiPO4 (PDF#15-0766) with cell parameters of a = 6.982 Å, b = 6.982 Å, and c = 6.476 Å. The stronger diffraction peaks at 2θ = 14.6°, 20.1°, 25.5°, 29.0°, 29.5°and 31.3° are indexed to (100), (101), (110), (111), (200) and (102) crystal planes of hexagonal BiPO4, respectively. What’s more, a weak diffraction peak at around 23°corresponding to the diffraction of CDs is observed in XRD pattern of CDs@BiPO4. Further characterizations of X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) also demonstrate the formation of CDs@BiPO4 (Fig. S4).

The UV–Vis absorption spectra of pure BiPO4 and CDs@BiPO4 were collected to evaluate their light harvesting properties. The suspension of phosphate group-modified CDs containing the same concentration with reaction solution for preparing CDs@BiPO4 mainly absorb the visible spectrum from 400 nm to 600 nm (Fig. S5a). It can be also demonstrated by the corresponding photoluminescence excitation (PLE) spectra, showing broad excitation band along with an optimal excitation wavelength of around 490 nm (Fig. S5b). As shown in Fig. 3 a, the pure BiPO4 displays the typical absorption edge at ~330 nm, which is similar with that of monoclinic BiPO4 [29,30]. After the incorporation of CDs into BiPO4, the absorbance of CDs@BiPO4 is significantly enhanced over the entire visible wavelength range due to the existence of the embedded CDs. However, the nanocomposite of CDs and monoclinic BiPO4 reported, which was synthesized via hydrothermal method [23], only show absorption at the range of from 300 to 450 nm. Using similar hydrothermal process, we also prepared a contrast sample (i.e. CDs@BiPO4 (180 °C)) at 180 °C and found that this sample indeed possesses lower visible absorbance than CDs@BiPO4 (Figs. S6 and S7). Besides the photosensitization of CDs, the broader and stronger visible absorption of CDs@BiPO4 could be also ascribed to the interface states induced by the combination of CDs with BiPO4 based on above contrast results [8]. Hereby our presented surface group-mediated nucleation reaction at room temperature promotes the generation of favorable interface states that can extend visible light absorption range.

Since CDs with excellent electron-transfer property are doped into the BiPO4 phase, we also expected the interface charge separation efficiency to be improved and hereby conduct the investigation of electrochemical impedance spectra (EIS). Phenomenally the smaller the arc radius, the higher the efficiency of charge transfer is [3]. From Fig. 3b, the EIS arc radius for CDs@BiPO4 descends tooneseventhofthe value ofpure BiPO4. As a result, the doping of CDs can strikingly enhance charge transfer efficiency of BiPO4, i.e. CDs embedded into BiPO4 can accelerate the migration and separation efficiency of photogenerated carriers. The photoinduced electron transfer properties were also studied using the well-known method reported broadly [31]. From Fig. 4 a, luminescence emission of phosphate-grafted CDs is quenched by known electron acceptors of 2,4-dinitrotoluene (DNT), whereas it can’t be quenched by known electron donors of N,N-diethylaniline (DEA). Consequently, phosphate-grafted CDs can only serve as electron donors. Thiscase isdistinctlydifferent from the previously reported result that photoexcited CDs are both excellent electron donors and excellent electron acceptors. To confirm it, we further used the Mott-Schottky equation to identify the type of conductivity of the phosphate-grafted CDs. Fig. 4b shows straight lines with positive slope located in different potential regimes. The positive slope corresponds to n-type conductivities, indicating that electron conduction is dominant in phosphate-grafted CDs.

The intrinsic reactive oxygen species (ROS), such as superoxide anion radical (
O2 −) and hydroxyl radical (
OH) in photocatalysis are generated by the reactions with the photoinduced electrons (e−) and holes (h+), which are considered to be involved in the actual oxidative and reductive reactions in catalytic effects [32]. Upon visible light excitation, photoelectrons are generated in CDs and then transferred on the surfaces of molecular oxygen O2.
O2 − is formed successively by reducing O2. Indeed,
O2 − has been detected by employing nitroblue tetrazolium (NBT) reduction method [32]. The yellow NBT is an indicator of reduction reactions and can change to blue formazan as a result of the reduction by
O2 −, which has been used to detect
O2 − in photocatalysis. Fig. 4c shows that the characteristic absorption peak almost disappears after adding CDs@BiPO4, whereas it has only a small decrease in pure BiPO4 due to the formation of insoluble blue formazan. Therefore, CDs@BiPO4 can generate more amount of
O2 − than pure BiPO4 on illumination. By using terephthalic acid (TA) as
OH probe [32,33], however, we confirmed the absence of
OH in detecting system of both pure BiPO4 and CDs@BiPO4, revealing no generation of photoinduced holes or no reaction caused by them under visible light irradiation.

Since UV absorption is only observed in pure BiPO4, the photoinduced holes should be difficult to be produced under visible light irradiation. Interestingly, why did CDs@BiPO4 with visible light absorption not induce the
OH formation? To uncover this issue, we determined the energy level positions of valence bands (VB) and conduction bands (CB) of pure BiPO4 and phosphate group-modified CDs using the XPS valence spectra together with band gap values and linear potential scans, respectively (Figs. S8 and S9). Fig. 4e gives the energy level diagrams of them. The VB and CB positions of phosphate-grafted CDs are higher than those of pure BiPO4. The transfer of photoinduced electrons and holes between CDs and BiPO4 occurs as illuminated in Fig. 4e. CDs not only act as electron donors, but as trapping agent of photogenerated holes because CDs are doped into the BiPO4. Thus, such hererostructure is good for catalytic reaction involved by photoinduced electrons.

Among various nitro compounds, the 4-nitrophenol (4-NP) which is toxic to the environment is one of the frequently occurring by-products. In pharmaceutical industries, the 4-aminophenol (4-AP) that is a vital precursor for the production of different medicines like paracetamol, phenacetin, acetanilide and so on, is derived from the 4-NP by reduction [34–36]. As a proof-of-concept, the prepared CDs@ BiPO4 was used for photocatalytic reduction of 4-NP to 4-AP in the aqueous phase under visible light irradiation. NaBH4 is not able to reduce 4-NP even after several days in absence of catalyst [37]. The reduction potential of 4-NP to 4-AP locates at around 0.7 V vs. NHE, which is lower CB position of BiPO4. In the case of enough reaction time, we found that the yellow solution of 4-NP can be changed to colorless whether CDs@ BiPO4 or pure BiPO4 is present. This is monitored by UV–Vis light absorption spectra, which displays a decreasing 4-NP ion peak at 400 nm and an increasing 4-AP peak at 300 nm [3,38]. As shown in Fig. 5 a-d, each sample can cause the same phenomenon that the maximum absorbance peak at 400 nm gradually declines as the reduction reaction time proceeds, accompanying a concomitant increase of the peak at 300 nm. For pure BiPO4, however, there are no obvious differences whether with visible irradiation or without visible irradiation. On the contrary, the catalytic activity of CDs@ BiPO4 is significantly higher under visible light irradiation than dark condition. It should be noted that the catalytic activity of CDs@ BiPO4 is similar with that of BiPO4 in dark condition. The degree of the conversion is provided by the ratio of the concentration (C) and the initial value of 4-NP (C 0), that is, C/C0 which is obtained by the corresponding absorbance at 400 nm according to the Lambert–Beer law. Pure BiPO4 shows no change of catalytic activity with and without visible irradiation (Fig. 5e), whereas the CDs@ BiPO4 displays significant difference in activity (Fig. 5f). Therefore, the catalytic activity of CDs@BiPO4 for 4-NP reduction can be remarkably enhanced by visible light irradiation. Taking into account all of measured conditions (e.g. the used amount of catalyst, the used concentration of 4-NP and NaBH4), the reduction activity of CDs@BiPO4 under visible light irradiation is actually superior to other fabricated catalytic materials reported in the literature such as resin-supported dye [34], porous Si nanoparticles containing with Pd and Fe2O3 nanoparticles [39].

In order to gain further insight into the mechanism of the photocatalysis, we also survey the impact of light intensity and wavelength on reaction rates from CDs@ BiPO4. Fig. 6 a shows the catalytic conversion rate of 4-NP to 4-AP as a function of visible light source intensity. Clearly, the photocatalytic rate exhibits a linear dependence on intensity up to 80 mWcm−2, but its increase becomes very slow above 80 mWcm−2 when irradiation time is 15 min. This nonlinear dependence reveals there is another controlled step that limits the proceeding of photoreduction reaction for 4-NP. In addition, the influence of incident light on the conversion rate of 4-NP to 4-AP was investigated using xenon light with wavelengths of 400, 500, 600 nm with normalized incident photon numbers, respectively. The studies found that the normalized photocatalytic rate of CDs@ BiPO4 decreases with wavelength increase of incident light (Fig. 6b).
(1)
T O F =
Amount
o f
4 N P ( μ m o l ) × c o n v e r s i o n ( % )
Mass
o f
c a t a l y s t
g
× r e a c t i o n
t i m e
( m i n )

The corresponding turnover frequency (TOF) of the conversion from 4-NP to 4-AP was also calculated by the Eq. (1) and given in Fig. 6c. All of above results are consistent with the trend of its absorbance as shown in Fig. 3a. The reduced photocatalytic activity is likely due to the decrease in the number of activated electrons generated at longer wavelength, confirming that the enhancement of reaction rate is driven by the light irradiation.

We also explored photocatalytic stability and recyclability of samples, which are key performance metrics for efficient and cost-effective industrial processes. Fig. 7 a reveals that the CDs@BiPO4 can be repeatedly used at least five times without evident deactivation for the reduction of 4-NP. By contrast, the photocatalytic activity of pure BiPO4 decreases distinctly after two cycles. To evaluate their structural stability, we measured XRD patterns and XPS spectra of CDs@BiPO4 and BiPO4 after reused five times (Fig. 7b–f). Comparing with fresh samples, there are no new peaks in XRD patterns of the used ones (Fig. 7b), attesting no new phase generation after repeatedly used five times. Furthermore, the new chemical compositions and obvious peak shifts are not observed in XPS full spectra after reused five times (Fig. 7c). By high-resolution XPS characterizations, the Bi 4f, O 1 s and P 2p spectra of the used CDs@BiPO4 are also unchanged, whereas these spectra of the used BiPO4 show a few shifts in comparison with the fresh ones (Figures d-f). This phenomenon verifies possibly that the structural stability and recyclability of CDs@BiPO4 is higher than that of pure BiPO4 during photocatalytic process in the presence of NaBH4.

The origins of the reduction of 4-NP by NaBH4 on the surface of catalyst can be elucidated by the well-known Langmuir-Hinshelwood model [40–42]. As shown in Fig. 8 a, the hydrogen species generated from borohydride ions are transferred to the surface of the BiPO4, and reagent molecules of 4-NP are simultaneously adsorbed on the same surface. Because the diffusion and adsorption/desorption of both reagents on the surface is reversible and fast [40,42], the rate-determining step is the reduction of the adsorbed 4-NP to 4-AP, which desorbs afterward fast. Therefore, the waste of surface hydrogen species in reaction process is not expected for reduction of 4-NP to 4-AP. We propose that the photoinduced electrons could boost the reduction reaction of surface hydrogen species (Fig. 8b). The photoinduced electrons are generated on illumination and then transferred to the surface of CDs@BiPO4. Because they possess higher energy level position than lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) of 4-NP, the photoinduced electrons will easily migrate to surface absorbed 4-NP molecules. This causes the activation of 4-NP and promotes their reduction through surface hydrogen species, leading to higher conversion rate and efficiency. As shown in Fig. 6a, the conversion rate will reach to a plateau due to the limitation of hydrogen specie amount when much more photons are offered.

On the other hand, the CDs@BiPO4 with larger BET surface area and more mesopores (Fig. S3) is favorable to the adsorption of the 4-NP molecules on its surface. The contrast experiments depicting adsorption phenomena are also performed for pure BiPO4 and CDs@BiPO4. As shown in Fig. S10, the adsorption amount of 4-NP on the surface of both samples increases with shaking time and remains constant after it reaches a maximum value of adsorption. Apparently, the adsorption capacity of CDs@BiPO4 is twice higher than that of pure BiPO4. The higher adsorption capacity is, the more efficient the utilization of surface hydrogen species is for the reduction of 4-NP. Hence, the CDs@BiPO4 shows better photocatalytic activity for 4-NP reduction in the presence of NaBH4.

A facile and green strategy for the fabrication of CDs@BiPO4 with a broad and strong visible light absorption was developed. The obtained sample exhibits a broadband absorption from 300 nm to 800 nm, no matter absorption intensity or visible absorption range it far exceeds the similar products prepared by energy-consuming hydrothermal method reported in literature. The photocatalytic efficiency of CDs@BiPO4 in the reduction of 4-NP to 4-AP is 2.5 times higher than that of the pure BiPO4 under visible light irradiation. In the heterostructure of CDs@BiPO4, CDs not only minish charge transfer impedance at the interface, but also act as electron donors and trapping agent of photogenerated holes, hereby resulting in higher charge separation and migration efficiency. The combination of the high adsorption capacity with the pre-activation of 4-NP induced by photoinduced electrons promotes highly efficient utilization of surface hydrogen species from borohydride ions. As a result, the CDs@BiPO4 displays excellent photocatalytic activity for 4-NP reduction in the presence of NaBH4.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. U1510125, 51502270, 21703209), Specialized Research Fund for Sanjin Scholars Program of Shanxi Province, Program for the Outstanding Innovative Teams of Higher Learning Institutions of Shanxi, and Transformation of Scientific and Technological Achievements Programs of Higher Education Institutions in Shanxi (TSTAP), North University of China Fund for Scientific Innovation Team.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cej.2019.123551.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Study of nucleation and growth mechanism of the metallic nanodumbbells

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: G. Krylova, L.J. Giovanetti, F.G. Requejo, N.M. Dimitrijevic, A. Prakapenka, E.V. Shevchenko, Study of Nucleation and Growth Mechanism of the Metallic Nanodumbbells, J. Am. Chem. Soc. 134 (2012) 4384-4392.
